20: A type: 'The man is a close relative of ours, one of our guardian-redeemers.' Boaz is the OT's most fully developed type of Christ as redeemer: (1) he is near of kin — Christ became our kinsman through the incarnation (Hebrews 2:14-15: he shared in flesh and blood that through death he might destroy him who has the power of death); (2) he has the right to redeem — as the sinless son of God, Christ alone qualifies; (3) he is willing to redeem — another go'el existed but declined (Ruth 4:6); Christ took on the obligation no one else could fulfill; (4) he pays the redemption price — Boaz redeems the land and takes Ruth; Christ redeems his people by his blood and takes them as his bride (Ephesians 5:25-27; Revelation 19:7-9). The kinsman-redeemer motif is the book of Ruth's entire Christological contribution.
1: A theme: Naomi has a kinsman (mōdaʿ), a man of standing (gibbôr ḥayil), whose name is Boaz. The redeemer is introduced before Ruth knows he exists. God’s provision is in place before those who need it discover it. Christ was “the Lamb slain from the foundation of the world” (Revelation 13:8) — the redemption was prepared before the fall that required it.
2: A theme: Ruth requests permission to glean — to live on the margins of another’s harvest, dependent on the owner’s generosity and the gleaning laws (Leviticus 19:9-10). Her humility in asking models the posture of those who approach Christ: coming to receive what they cannot produce, acknowledging dependence on another’s abundance. The one who has nothing comes to the one who has everything, and receives.
3: A revelation of God: “As it happened, she came to the part of the field belonging to Boaz.” The narrator flags divine providence operating through apparent coincidence. God’s hidden direction governs the seemingly random encounter — the same pattern as the Ethiopian eunuch on the Gaza road (Acts 8:26-27), the woman at the well (John 4:4: Jesus “had to pass through Samaria”), Zacchaeus in the sycamore tree (Luke 19:4-5).
4: A theme: Boaz greets workers with “The LORD be with you” — they respond in kind. The man of hesed names YHWH in ordinary speech; his workplace is sabbath-shaped. Christ brings the character of God into every human encounter: “full of grace and truth” (John 1:14). The blessing exchanged between Boaz and his workers models the community of the redeemed who bless one another in YHWH’s name.
5: A theme: Boaz asks “Whose young woman is this?” The redeemer inquires after the one who will become his own. This seeking posture mirrors Christ’s initiative: “The Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost” (Luke 19:10). The redeemer’s question precedes the redeemed’s petition.
6: A theme: the foreman identifies Ruth as “the Moabite.” Her foreign identity is stated first. Boaz knows she is an outsider before he speaks to her — and speaks anyway. Christ similarly chose to extend covenant belonging to those whose exclusion from it was legally established (Ephesians 2:12-13: those who were “without God in the world” are brought near through his blood).
7: A theme: Ruth asks to glean even among the sheaves — beyond what the law minimally guaranteed. She asks for more than her legal entitlement. Those who approach Christ with expectant petition receive beyond what the law can provide: “abundantly more than all we ask or think” (Ephesians 3:20). The posture of asking rather than assuming is met with excess.
8: A theme: Boaz addresses Ruth as “my daughter” — covenant kinship language extended to a stranger. The redeemer speaks belonging before the legal adoption is finalized. Christ addresses those still distant with the vocabulary of family (Matthew 25:34: “Come, you who are blessed by my Father, inherit the kingdom”), calling them children before the full adoption is complete.
9: A theme: Boaz provides water before Ruth asks. “When you are thirsty, go to the vessels and drink from what the young men have drawn.” The redeemer anticipates thirst before it is voiced. Christ identifies himself as the answer to universal thirst: “If anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink” (John 7:37); “whoever drinks of the water I give will never be thirsty again” (John 4:14). Every drink of water given to the weary points toward the living water.
10: A theme: Ruth falls with her face to the ground: “Why have I found favor in your eyes, since I am a foreigner?” The right response to unexpected grace is prostrate wonder and honest self-assessment. The centurion’s declaration — “Lord, I am not worthy to have you come under my roof” (Matthew 8:8) — is the same posture. Those who recognize they have no claim on the redeemer’s grace are in the right position to receive it.
11: A revelation of God: Boaz has heard everything Ruth did for Naomi. The redeemer already knows the story of the one he is about to redeem. Christ’s knowledge of his own is complete before they approach him: “I know my own” (John 10:14); “Before I formed you in the womb I knew you” (Jeremiah 1:5). The omniscience of the redeemer makes his grace more personal, not less.
12: A type: “May the LORD repay you... under whose wings you have come to take refuge.” Boaz prays that YHWH will give Ruth what she has sought — and in Ruth 3 he becomes the answer when Ruth asks him to spread his garment (kānāp, same word as “wing”) over her. The redeemer prays for what he will himself provide. Christ intercedes for his people (Hebrews 7:25; John 17) and is himself the shelter he prays they will find.
13: A theme: Ruth says she has been comforted and spoken to kindly, “though I am not one of your servants.” She has received belonging-language while her legal status is still “outsider.” This is the grammar of grace: treated as belonging before the adoption is legally complete. Paul captures the same paradox in Ephesians 2:19: those who were “strangers and aliens” are “now fellow citizens with the saints and members of the household of God.”
14: A shadow: Boaz invites Ruth to share bread and vinegar at mealtime with the reapers. Table fellowship extended to the outsider anticipates the eucharistic table where Christ invites those far off to eat and drink with him. The messianic banquet (Isaiah 25:6; Luke 14:15-24) reaches its institutional form in the Lord’s Supper; every anticipation of the redeemer sharing his table with the excluded is a foretaste.
15: A theme: Boaz grants Ruth gleaning access even among the sheaves — beyond the minimum the law requires — and tells the young men not to rebuke her. The redeemer gives not just provision but safety; not just access but welcome. Grace exceeds the request and adds protection. This pattern of excess governs Christ’s giving: “full grace upon grace” (John 1:16).
16: A theme: Boaz instructs his men to drop stalks deliberately for her — provision hidden within what appears to be natural gleaning. The dignifying grace that maintains the recipient’s agency rather than making charity obvious mirrors the God who gives in ways that honor those who receive: “do not let your left hand know what your right hand is doing” (Matthew 6:3). Grace does not humiliate; it honors.
17: A theme: Ruth gleans an ephah of barley — an extraordinary amount by any gleaning standard, approximately a month’s supply for two people. The redeemer’s field yields beyond expectation. The twelve baskets of fragments after feeding five thousand (Matthew 14:20), the hundredfold harvest (Mark 4:20), the wine abundance at Cana (John 2:6-10) — each is a variation on the theme that the redeemer’s provision exceeds every measure of need.
18: A theme: Ruth returns to Naomi carrying not only the gleaned grain but the leftover food from the meal Boaz gave her — she shares what the redeemer gave her. The NT’s economic ethic is built on this pattern: those who have received grace share it (Acts 4:32-35). The overflow of the redeemer’s provision is not hoarded but distributed through the community of the redeemed.
19: A theme: Naomi blesses the unknown man before she knows it is Boaz. The recognition sequence — experience grace, then discover the source — mirrors conversion: people encounter the grace of God before they know its name. The Emmaus disciples recognize Christ in the breaking of bread (Luke 24:30-31) after he has already opened the Scriptures to them. The revelation of the redeemer’s name follows the experience of his grace.
21: A theme: Boaz’s invitation covers the whole harvest season — not a one-day provision but sustained belonging through the full duration of the work. Christ’s provision is similarly durable: “I am with you always, to the end of the age” (Matthew 28:20); “he who began a good work in you will bring it to completion” (Philippians 1:6). The grace that begins the harvest does not abandon at midpoint.
22: A theme: Naomi’s counsel — stay in Boaz’s field, do not go to another — is the wisdom of proximity to the redeemer. Those who drift from the community of the redeemed expose themselves to harm. The NT’s warnings about drifting (Hebrews 2:1; Ruth 10:25; 1 John 2:19) carry the same structural logic: remain close to the one who provides shelter; do not wander into fields where no one protects you.
23: A theme: Ruth gleans through both the barley and wheat harvests — the full grain season, from Passover to Pentecost. The Spirit is poured out at Pentecost (the wheat harvest feast), producing the harvest of the first converts of the new covenant community (Acts 2). Ruth’s endurance through both harvests is a quiet figure for the covenant community that endures from the Passover of the cross to the Pentecost of the Spirit.
